coredata.dc:title: Solar H2O2 production with carbon dots and nickel co-modified titania nanotube photocatalyst stabilized at water/benzyl alcohol interface
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Yingxu
Zhang, Guan
coredata.dc:description: An interfacial reaction system with the advantage of in situ H2O2 production and separation has been developed, in which titania nanotube (TNT) photocatalyst modified by nickel species and carbon dots (CDs) can self-stabilize at interface of water/benzyl alcohol. Under visible light irradiation, the CDs/Ni-TNT yields H2O2 with an initial rate of 385.6 μmol/g/h. The improved activity could be attributed to multiple effects such as sufficient oxygen adsorption and mass transfer, separated reduction and oxidation sites at different phases, and rapid diffusion of H2O2 from the interfacial region into bulk aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Composite materials
Green synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of carbon dots and its size distribution (inset); (b) UV–visible absorbance spectra of carbon dots dissolved in water and images of aqueous sample in water and solid sample (inset); (c) and (d) HR-TEM images of CDs/Ni-TNT sample.
(a) XRD patterns; (b) UV–visible absorbance spectra; (c) Fluorescence spectra of different TNT samples; XPS spectra of the CDs/Ni-TNT sample: (d) C 1s, (e) Ti 2p and (f) O 2p.

H2O2 production at different conditions: (a) effect of CDs ratio; (b) yield of H2O2 at different phases; (c) effect of water volume; (d) effect of irradiation wavelength; (e) recycle experiments; (f) outdoor experiments for bacteria inactivation.
Proposed self-assembled interfacial system for photocatalytic H2O2 production (left panel) and reaction mechanism of H2O2 production (right panel).

head.ce:title: Solar H2O2 production with carbon dots and nickel co-modified titania nanotube photocatalyst stabilized at water/benzyl alcohol interface

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Organic Pollution Prevention and Control, School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Organic Pollution Prevention and Control
School of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Organic Pollution Prevention and Control, School of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, Shenzhen 518055, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An interfacial reaction system with the advantage of in situ H2O2 production and separation has been developed, in which titania nanotube (TNT) photocatalyst modified by nickel species and carbon dots (CDs) can self-stabilize at interface of water/benzyl alcohol. Under visible light irradiation, the CDs/Ni-TNT yields H2O2 with an initial rate of 385.6 μmol/g/h. The improved activity could be attributed to multiple effects such as sufficient oxygen adsorption and mass transfer, separated reduction and oxidation sites at different phases, and rapid diffusion of H2O2 from the interfacial region into bulk aqueous solution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a great need for development of cost-effective technology for H2O2 production and separation [1]. Among various methods, the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 has emerged as a prominent research topic in recent years [2–4]. Among these photocatalysts, TiO2 has garnered significant attention due to its stable performance, low cost, non-toxic nature, and harmless characteristics. However, TiO2 also faces challenges related to low solar light utilization and low charge separation efficiency [5–7]. While current efforts are primarily focused on enhancing the performance of photocatalytic materials [8], it is imperative not to overlook the exploration of advanced photocatalytic systems for improved H2O2 synthesis and separation [9].

Recently, there has been a significant focus on three-phase interface engineering for advanced catalytic applications [10]. Enhanced photocatalytic reactions, such as the degradation of organic pollutants, H2 production, and H2O2 synthesis at air–liquid-solid joint interfaces, have been reported [11–14]. Inspired by these findings, it is encouraging that designing an interfacial reaction system for H2O2 synthesis would be attractive.

This study aims to establish a water-catalyst-oil interfacial reaction system as illustrated in Scheme 1 . The photocatalyst nanoparticles are able to self-assemble and remain suspended at the interface due to electrostatic force and buoyancy. This arrangement will facilitate the oxygen reduction reaction in the water phase and hole oxidation reaction in the oil phase, respectively. The resulting H2O2 can rapidly diffuse into the aqueous phase, thereby facilitating its separation and application in subsequent stages. Building upon this concept, TiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized into TiO2 nanotubes (TNT) to enhance light carrier separation and oxygen adsorption transfer ability. Subsequently, inexpensive transition metal (nickel) deposition was carried out as a co-catalyst to improve charge transfer rate. Additionally, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were employed to modify TNT for expanded visible light absorption capacity and enhanced H2O2 production efficiency.

TiO2 nanotubes were synthesized based on the alkaline transformation of titanium dioxide using a hydrothermal method [15]. Subsequently, Ni was deposited onto TNT by reducing Ni(NO3)2 with NaBH4 under alkaline conditions with mass ratio of 0.5 wt%. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method [16]. Subsequently, specific ratios of CDs (1.0–10.0 wt%) and Ni-TNT were added to an absolute ethanol volume of 20 mL and stirred in a water bath. Finally, the dry powder obtained above was further dried overnight at 60 °C to achieve different mass ratios of CDs/Ni-TNT as desired. The detailed information on the catalysts preparation was described in Supporting Information.

5 mL of benzyl alcohol was added to the reactor, followed by the slow addition of 35 mL of water (pH = 3.0) to form a two-phase system. Subsequently, the photocatalyst was gradually added from above and allowed to stabilize at the interface of water/alcohol. Air purging into the water layer at a rate of 15 mL/min and visible light irradiation from a xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm) were then performed to initiate the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2. The H2O2 concentration in the solution was calculated using the DPD-POD method [16]. The detailed information on the bacteria inactivation and catalysts characterization was described in Supporting Information.

The CDs with sizes ranging from a few nanometers were successfully prepared and are evenly distributed in aqueous solution without obvious agglomeration (Fig. 1 a). The diameter of the prepared carbon quantum dots ranged between 1 and 3 nm (Fig. 1a inset). As shown in Fig. 1b, CDs displayed two distinct absorption peaks at 240 and 330 nm. Furthermore, they exhibited strong visible light absorbance within wavelengths ranging from 400-450 nm extending into 700 nm. The homogeneous green-colored CDs solution was observed in the inset of Fig. 1b; after undergoing frozen-dry processing, light-green colored CDs powder was obtained.

As illustrated in Fig. 1c and d, the nanotubes exhibited an inner diameter of 3.0–5.0 nm and a wall thickness of approximately 1.0–1.5 nm, confirming the successful preparation of TNT from commercial TiO2 (P25). Additionally, some CDs and Ni-based nanoparticles were directly observed on the surface of TNT, indicating successful co-modification with CDs and Ni species. Given the process for reducing and depositing Ni2+ was conducted under alkaline conditions, it is more likely that chemical precipitation occurred resulting in the formation of Ni(OH)2 on TNT.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, different TiO2 samples displayed main diffraction peaks at angles corresponding to anatase phase; a small peak at 27.5° attributed to rutile phase was observed for the Ni-TNT sample. The TNT did not show a significant change in light absorption capacity compared to commercial TiO2. Both materials exhibited an absorption band only in the ultraviolet region (Fig. 2b). However, CDs/Ni-TNT showed significant visible light absorption at 400–800 nm, suggesting that the loading of CDs significantly expanded the Ni-TNT absorption into the visible light region.

The fluorescence intensity of Ni-TNT is greater than that of TNT, which may be attributed to both a higher probability of recombination and lower photocarrier yield leading to increased recombination rates (Fig. 2c). After CDs loading, the fluorescence signals from the CDs/Ni-TNT composite were significantly quenched. The reduced photoluminescence suggests that binding between Ni-TNT and CDs effectively promotes charge separation and transfer while inhibiting recombination of photocarriers [17]. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2d, the enhanced C 1s signal on CDs/Ni-TNT compared with Ni-TNT reasonably ascribed to the presence of CDs. In contrast to Ni-TNT, loading CDs caused negative shifts in Ti peaks (Fig. 2e), suggesting an interaction between CDs and TNT surface elements. Ni-TNT and CDs/Ni-TNT exhibit O 1s peaks at 529.8 and 531.7 eV (Fig. 2f), corresponding to TiO and OH groups, respectively. Additionally, the peaks at 531.6 eV are attributed to the joint contribution of TiO group and CO group in CDs.

Ni-TNT exhibits minimal production of H2O2 under visible light at λ > 420 nm in the absence of CDs (Fig. 3 a). However, the yield of synthesized H2O2 increases with the CDs load ranging from 1 % to 5 %. The yield can reach to 96 μmol/L when the mass ratio of CDs is 5 %. Nevertheless, as the load of CDs continues to increase, there is a decrease in H2O2 yield due to excessive agglomeration. Notably, when comparing with other systems, H2O2 production by the best CDs/Ni-TNT photocatalyst significantly decreased when suspended in solvent, highlighting advantages associated with interfacial systems for H2O2 production. As illustrated in Fig. 3b, no H2O2 production was observed in aqueous phase; however, 18.3 μmol/L of H2O2 was produced in oil phase, accounting for approximately 18.9 % of total yield. This result demonstrates that H2O2 production mainly originated from interfacial photocatalyst rather than the photocatalyst suspended in water or benzyl alcohol.

As depicted in Fig. 3c, a decrease in the aqueous phase volume from 35 mL to 20 mL resulted in an increase in H2O2 concentration, indicating that reducing the aqueous phase volume is a viable approach for achieving higher concentrations of H2O2. However, further reduction in water volume led to a decrease in H2O2 concentration. The reduction in water phase volume increases perturbation influence on the interface between two phases due to increased aeration. In Fig. 3d, CDs/Ni-TNT exhibited varied H2O2 yields under different visible light wavelengths, particularly when irradiation wavelength was less than 475 nm, shorter wavelengths resulted in higher H2O2 production.

As shown in Fig. 3e, the H2O2 yield was 101.26 μmol/L under visible light irradiation in the initial test, and subsequent four cycles exhibited a decreasing trend. The decline in performance can be attributed to the gradual loss of CDs from the surface of CDs/Ni-TNT, oxidation of CDs during photocatalytic reactions, and catalyst agglomeration. To demonstrate potential application of this system for H2O2 photosynthesis and utilization under natural solar light irradiation, the produced H2O2 solution was directly used to deactivate bacteria (E. coli). Approximately 75 % of E. coli (initial concentration = 105 CFU/mL) were deactivated following a dark reaction period of 60 min when using H2O2 generated from the solar photocatalytic process (Fig. 3f).

The proposed interfacial reaction system enhanced photocatalytic production and separation of H2O2, as demonstrated by using CDs/Ni-TNT photocatalyst as an example. It has many advantages for H2O2 production compared to conventional slurry reaction systems, such as separated reduction and oxidation at different phases and efficient separation of H2O2 from the photocatalyst. This proposed H2O2 production system could be applied to various types of photocatalysts and easily scaled up for large-scale production and application under natural solar light irradiation.
Yingxu Xie: Investigation, Formal analysis. Guan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Commission (No. JCYJ20210316074858001; RCYX20210609103122038).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.137147.
